text: @manhdungYokiko are you claiming photonics is not profitable?
@SebKuhnert some accounts think there is only the photonics sector and tech sebastian
@Denis_13_1982 @aleabitoreddit its almost like there are sectors outside photonics to invest in.  Very strange.

This is super interesting from @MarvellTech 

Plasmonics era beckons

- Plasmonic-based Silicon Photonics light engines could support modules operating at 3.2T and beyond with lesser space and lower power per bit.

- SiPho modulators, while highly scalable and cost-efficient, are fundamentally constrained by the physical properties of light modulation in silicon, limiting their ability to scale efficiently to higher bandwidths.

- Plasmonic waveguides address these challenges by compressing light below the diffraction limit at a metal-dielectric interface. 

- These structures are integrated directly onto the silicon photonics platform, enabling the industry to build on the extensive foundation already established with SiPho while incorporating the performance advantages of plasmonics.

- Plasmonic waveguides consist of a metal-dielectric channel filled with an active Pockels material integrated into SiPho devices or other photonic material system. Thus, Plasmonic waveguides can respond much faster to electrical signals, enabling much higher modulation rates than conventional photonic modulators

- The technology is still in the early days of development for commercialization but lots of work required from scale manufacturing perspective.

$MRVL $LITE $COHR

I honestly think that $SIVE and $LPK sold out so hard today because @aleabitoreddit  posted favorably of memory and said photonics has been down and a bunch of lemmings went and dumped to go buy memory at the top.

It's probably better if we don't have such holders among us. 🤦‍♂️

Just seriously stupid and rash and I suspect lots who did it are not English speakers.

text:
🧵 The Cosmic Virtual Metasurface: TSFTR-NF in the Lab
What if the universe isn’t a mechanical machine, but a software-defined reality?
A major breakthrough just published in Advanced Photonics Nexus by researchers at Nottingham Trent University didn't just revolutionize optics—it accidentally provided a real-world proof of concept for the Temporal‑Spatial Frame Theory of Reality (TSFTR‑NF).
Here is how cutting-edge laboratory photonics just validated a fractal network universe: 👇
1/ The Analog-to-Digital Shift
•In the Lab: Scientists discarded rigid, physical micro-lenses for "virtual metasurfaces." Using a Spatial Light Modulator (SLM), they project 2D digital patterns of light that update every 20ms, changing the lens's entire function on the fly.
•In TSFTR-NF: We discard smooth, continuous spacetime. Reality is a decentralized network of localized, discrete "frames" (Δt=tp ) that dynamically "render" and assemble themselves based on local causal interactions.
2/ 2D Holographic Projection
•In the Lab: The team used a flat, 2D emulated optical pattern to manipulate light in 3D space—focusing, steering, and changing its color concurrently without moving physical parts.
•In TSFTR-NF: Our framework relies on scale-dependent dimensionality. At the Planck scale, the spectral dimension (ds ) drops to 2. Spacetime emerges macroscopically because 2D quantum boundary threads weave together to project our 4D macro reality.
3/ Turning the Invisible Visible
•In the Lab: The breakthrough achievement was using a programmable light pattern to instantly convert invisible infrared wavelengths into visible light that a standard, cheap camera could capture, adjusting focal length on demand.
•In TSFTR-NF: This is a literal manifestation of our Reality Index R(r). By altering the local density of the network connections, the metasurface reshapes how information scales from boundary to volume. It proves that "matter" and "wavelengths" are just emergent properties of the underlying information network.
The Macro Conclusion
The Nottingham Trent team realized that to truly master light, you have to stop building rigid physical hardware and start programming the medium itself.
TSFTR-NF argues that nature figured this out at the Big Bang. The universe isn't a rigid clockwork canvas; it is a self-assembling, holographic fractal network. We are living inside a cosmic virtual metasurface.
The lab is finally catching up to the framework.
Shoutout to the global research teams pushing optics into the digital age! 📐✨

text:
🚨 השוק כבר לא קונה סיפורי AI.
הוא קונה את צוואר הבקבוק הבא.
בשנתיים האחרונות המשקיעים חיפשו את מודל ה-AI הבא.היום, השוק כבר מסתכל עמוק יותר.הוא מחפש את החברות שבלעדיהן אי אפשר יהיה להמשיך לבנות את מהפכת ה-AI.השבוע קיבלנו לכך חיזוק משמעותי.$MU הוכיחה שהביקוש לזיכרון AI ממשיך להיות חזק, עם חוזים ארוכי טווח שמחזקים את הוודאות העסקית.$QCOM הציגה אסטרטגיה ברורה להפוך משחקנית מובילה בסמארטפונים לחברה שמכוונת גם ל-Edge AI, מחשוב מתקדם ותשתיות AI.אבל מבחינתי...אלה לא החדשות המרכזיות.החדשות הן שהן מסמנות איפה נמצא צוואר הבקבוק הבא.בכל פעם שתעשיית ה-AI פותרת בעיה אחת...נוצרת בעיה חדשה.התקדמנו ממחסור בכוח מחשוב...למחסור בזיכרון.מזיכרון...לקישוריות.מקישוריות...לפוטוניקה.מפוטוניקה...לאריזות שבבים.ומשם...לציוד ייצור.לחשמל.לקירור.לדאטה סנטרים.ולבסוף...לחומרי הגלם והמינרלים הקריטיים שעליהם כל שרשרת הערך נשענת.לכן אני לא מחפש רק את מניית ה-AI הבאה.אני מחפש את החברות שפותרות את צוואר הבקבוק הבא.כי לשם, בדרך כלל, זורם ההון.כך אני מסתכל על השוק.לא דרך הכותרות.אלא דרך שרשרת הערך של ה-AI.מי שמבין איפה נוצר צוואר הבקבוק הבא, עשוי להבין גם איפה ייווצר הערך הבא.הטיקרים שאני עוקב אחריהם בתחום:
$MU $QCOM $NVDA $AVGO $MRVL $AMD $TSM $ASML $AMAT $LRCX $KLAC $WOLF $VRT $SMCI $ORCL $ANET $CRWV $ARM $MTSI $POET
#AI #ArtificialIntelligence #PhysicalAI #EdgeAI #Semiconductors #Memory #HBM #Photonics #DataCenters #Infrastructure #ChipStocks #TechStocks #StockMarket #Investing #WallStreet #LongTermInvesting #FutureTech #AIInfrastructure #ALMI #AviLeviTradesאין באמור המלצה לקנייה או למכירה של ניירות ערך. כל האמור הוא לצורכי לימוד והעשרה בלבד.

text:
$SIVE occupies a critical, highly leveraged position within the AI hardware ecosystem by architectural integration, serving as a vital enabler for the massive data streams generated by $MU advanced memory solutions. While $SIVE does not supply physical components directly into $MU High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) packages, its strategic collaborations with $MXL and optical innovators like Ayar Labs position it to capture outsized financial gains as Micron scales production.

In essence, $MU manufactures the hyper-fast data engines, while $SIVE provides the ultra-high-bandwidth optical and radio-frequency (RF) conduits required to keep those engines from bottlenecking.

Through its Sivers Wireless division, the company has established a deep co-development and reference design partnership with $MXL.

This collaboration merges $SIVE proprietary millimeter-wave (mmWave) RFIC transceivers with $MXL high-performance baseband modems to create unified, low-latency transmission platforms capable of exceeding 10 Gbps data rates.

When $MU secures record-breaking enterprise contracts for its HBM3E or HBM4 architectures, hyperscalers simultaneously scale their entire data center networking fabric to handle the massive influx of data.

$MXL captures this demand by deploying its specialized connectivity and storage accelerator platforms into the AI server racks.

Because $SIVE hardware is natively integrated into $MXL blueprints, every infrastructure win for MaxLinear triggers a direct cascade of high-volume component orders for Sivers, converting $MU macro-driven growth into tangible product revenue for the smaller chipmaker.

The most explosive long-term financial catalyst for $SIVE, however, lies in its Sivers Photonics division and its direct contribution to the optical computing revolution.

$MU next-generation memory layers process data at speeds that legacy copper wiring can no longer support without generating catastrophic thermal throttling and severe power degradation. $SIVE addresses this critical industry pain point by manufacturing advanced InP (Indium Phosphide) DFB laser arrays.

These specialized lasers act as the foundational light source for silicon photonics, transforming electronic data into high-speed light signals.

$SIVE feeds these laser arrays directly to Ayar Labs, an industry-standard optical interconnect pioneer backed heavily by $NVDA, $AMD, and $INTC, which integrates them into "TeraPHY" optical chiplets.

These chiplets sit directly alongside high-performance compute modules and memory stacks, allowing $MU HBM to communicate with GPUs at the speed of light.

Coupled with $SIVE strategic partnership with $GFS to embed these laser architectures directly into commercial AI foundry wafers, Sivers is transitioning from an engineering-services firm into a high-margin, high-volume hardware supplier. s.

text:
Reality check:

• Not a quantum paper. No qubits, no computing read-across.
• Bandwidth and energy are deliberately weak vs laser-based links.
• Durability degrades steadily; the polymer roughens and scatters over billions of cycles.
• Benchtop optics and a single foundry demo, not a fielded product.

But zoom out:

The picks-and-shovels story for microscale robots and implants keeps maturing. A sub-cent, CMOS-compatible uplink removes a real bottleneck for mass-manufactured microsystems.

Direction: the microrobot and bioelectronics stack is quietly getting cheaper to scale, the same pattern photonics is driving for quantum.

user_name: 𝕂𝕆𝕁𝕀🇺🇸決算と経済指標💎新NISA完全対応🏄🌊
text:
🔦CWレーザー争奪戦、光の電源を誰が握るのか
AIデータセンターの話をしていると、どうしても主役はNVIDIAのGPUになる。

それは自然だ。画面に出てくる数字も派手だし、決算のたびに市場が見ているのもGPUの出荷、粗利率、ラック単位の供給能力だ。GPUが足りない。HBMが足りない。電力が足りない。冷却が足りない。毎月どこかで新しい詰まりどころが出てくる。

ただ、ある時から別のものが妙に気になり始めた。

光だ。

もっと正確に言えば、シリコンフォトニクスやCPOの中で使われるCWレーザーだ。GPU同士をつなぐネットワークのさらに奥で、信号を光に変えるための連続した光源である。

このテーマを一言で言うなら、AI工場の「光の電源」を誰が押さえるのか、という話になる。

AIデータセンターと聞くと、読者がまず思い浮かべるのはGPU、HBM、電力設備、冷却装置だと思う。しかし巨大なAIを動かす時、演算能力だけでは足りない。GPU同士が大量のデータをやり取りできなければ、せっかくの演算機が待ち時間を抱える。そこで800G、1.6T、さらにCPOという流れが出てくる。

この記事では、CWレーザーを単独の部品としてではなく、サプライチェーン全体で見る。材料、基板、エピタキシャル成長、レーザーチップ、外部光源モジュール、シリコンフォトニクス、量産テスト。それぞれの工程で、どの銘柄のエクスポージャーが高いのかを分けて整理する。

読むポイントは三つある。

CWレーザーは「レーザー会社だけ」の話ではないことInP基板、エピウェハ、化合物半導体ファウンドリ、光結合、熱制御、検査装置まで広がる。

直接度と事業インパクトを分けることLumentumやCoherentは一次情報が強い。SiversやAAOIはテーマ純度が高い。一方でSoitecやTowerは、レーザーではなくシリコンフォトニクス側の受け皿として効く。

まだ確定していない話を確定したように扱わないことNVIDIAがLumentumとCoherentに投資し、購入コミットメントと将来キャパアクセス権を押さえたことは確認できる。しかし、AMDや各クラウド事業者がどのCWレーザー会社とどんな長期契約を結んだかは、一次情報では限定的だ。

では、なぜ今この地味な部品が市場の関心を集めるのか。

ぼくはこれを「光源の席取りゲーム」と見ている。

🟨GPUの次に詰まる場所

AIデータセンターを大きな工場にたとえると、GPUは作業員だ。HBMは作業員の机の上に置かれた高速なメモ帳。電力は工場を動かす電源。冷却は熱くなった機械を止めないための空調だ。

ではネットワークは何か。

工場内の道路である。

どれだけ優秀な作業員を集めても、材料を運ぶ道路が細ければ工場は詰まる。作業員が増えるほど、道路の混雑は目立つ。AIデータセンターでも同じことが起きる。GPUを大量に並べるほど、GPU間の通信量が増え、従来の電気配線だけでは距離、損失、発熱、消費電力が厳しくなる。

そこで光接続が前に出る。800Gから1.6Tへ進み、次に3.2Tが見えてくる。スイッチや光エンジンの近くに光部品を置くCPO、つまり同一パッケージ内に光を近づける考え方も強まる。

NVIDIAは2025年3月にSpectrum-X PhotonicsとQuantum-X Photonicsを発表し、CPOとシリコンフォトニクスをAI工場の拡張技術として位置づけた。発表では、従来方式に比べてレーザー数を4分の1にし、電力効率を高め、信号品質やネットワーク耐障害性を改善するとうたっている。

この話の面白いところは、派手な主役が変わるわけではない点だ。GPUは依然として主役である。ただ、GPUが主役であり続けるために、脇役だった光部品が急に舞台袖から中央へ押し出される。

スポーツで言えば、スーパースターの得点力ばかり語っていたら、実はチームの勝敗を決めていたのはパスを通すポイントガードだった、というやつだ。派手なダンクはない。でもそこが止まると、全員が止まる。

CWレーザーは、そのポイントガードに近い。

Secteur photonique :

Goulots d’Étranglement Actuels / À Venir (2026-2028)
1.  Capacité de production InP / GaAs wafers — Très limitée, lead times longs.
2.  Packaging & Test avancé (Co-Packaged Optics) — Complexité thermique, alignement précis, yields bas.
3.  Matières premières — Indium, Gallium, Germanium (contrôles à l’export Chine).
4.  Énergie & Eau pour les fabs et data centers.
5.  Main-d’œuvre qualifiée et savoir-faire en photonics (très concentré).
Sources principales : Earnings calls NVIDIA/Broadcom 2025-2026, rapports SemiAnalysis, Omdia, Deloitte Semiconductor Outlook 2026, investissements CHIPS Act dans InP.

text:
This is a really solid take...

For photonics specifically, your emotions toward it right now are highly dependent on which companies you are in

For many months every single name in this batch caught bids indiscriminately. Many of them (in my opinion) didn't deserve to. But momentum is momentum!

Now you are seeing some of that momentum unwind.

And if you are heavy in those positions, it could be nerve-racking

But if you take a step back you can also see it as opportunity

If you believe in the optical supercycle and believe we are still in the early stages on the physical buildout, then you understand that money will flow into those companies that the market believes will be durable

Thoughts?

Photonics, energy, power semiconductors, memory. And amid all the talk of bottlenecks, it is easy to forget a simpler constraint. Capital is not infinite either. A stock that merely carried the label of its momentum sector, that bore the name but felt none of the momentum in its actual operations, will not keep pace under these conditions. When several strong themes compete for the same limited pool of capital, the money is allocated more efficiently. It no longer floods every corner of a hot sector. It seeks out the names that genuinely earn it. So ask yourself once more whether the animal in your stable is a racehorse or merely a donkey. Yesterday you could see it plainly, as a great deal of capital flowed into memory stocks. $LITE outperformed a $SIVE. $XFAB underperformed all of them. Same stable, very different animals. The weak narratives will have a harder time of it now. When capital was abundant and concentrated in a single theme, a thin story could still hitch a ride on the broader enthusiasm. With money spread across four competing sectors and allocated with more discipline, there is little left over for a stock whose only claim is the label it wears. Membership is no longer the point. text:
Optics/Photonics theme topped around mid to late May, and it has been one of the worst-performing groups in the market. The downturn was led by some of the most volatile stocks in the market.

These are levels that need to be held, otherwise it could get really ugly. 

At least a bounce is needed right around current levels, as they are getting super oversold.
1. $POET -55%
2. $AXTI: -53%
3. $AAOI -45%

text:
Market Review 20260626
- Tug of war between bulls and bears still in progress. Big sell off in Tech (especially AI hardware related). Think $BE $CRDO etc. Photonics not much better, only GLW holding well. 
- Money is leaving: Semis (sorta), Photonics, DataCentres, etc Mag7
- Money is entering: Healthcare, Biotech, Cybersecurity, Selected Fintech, Selected Software
- Big question in my mind is, will money be leaving Memory soon? That is the only theme really holding up the tech sector now. WDC and STX leading the way down today. $MU  and SNDK not far behind. There was a subtle tell after MU earnings. After a sympathy gap up with MU, WDC sold off hard on Thursday, and then continued with ferocity today. 
- I've been wary of the AI Hardware trade for a while now. We were big beneficiaries of the ramp up in Q2 but I've largely been cautious on them since early June. Will that positioning finally be vindicated? Looking at the way BE and CRDO acted today! 2 consecutive days of big selling, and on huge volume too. If I flipped the chart around I would be a big buyer going forward with the right setups. It also triggers what is commonly called an Initial Price Movement.  
- And then the memories, I do think we made the right call to avoid them FOR THE TIME BEING. Let them pull back, build a base and if the right setup comes along, we can participate again. I still believe in the AI buildout story, but playing it differently now. 
- When will that happen? I have no idea. Watch this space and stocks showing up in the etoro portfolio if /when I buy them. 
- Been a choppy week and I expect it to continue, with a bias that we might start a short term downtrend (ie not just sideways). Coming Monday's price action might be the tell. 
- All the best.

Big sell off in Tech (especially AI hardware related). Think $BE $CRDO etc. Photonics not much better, only GLW holding well. - Money is leaving: Semis (sorta), Photonics, DataCentres, etc Mag7 - Money is entering: Healthcare, Biotech, Cybersecurity, Selected Fintech, Selected Software - Big question in my mind is, will money be leaving Memory soon? That is the only theme really holding up the tech sector now. WDC and STX leading the way down today. $MU and SNDK not far behind. There was a subtle tell after MU earnings. After a sympathy gap up with MU, WDC sold off hard on Thursday, and then continued with ferocity today. - I've been wary of the AI Hardware trade for a while now. We were big beneficiaries of the ramp up in Q2 but I've largely been cautious on them since early June. Will that positioning finally be vindicated? Looking at the way BE and CRDO acted today! 2 consecutive days of big selling, and on huge volume too. If I flipped the chart around I would be a big buyer going forward with the right setups. It also triggers what is commonly called an Initial Price Movement. - And then the memories, I do think we made the right call to avoid them FOR THE TIME BEING. Let them pull back, build a base and if the right setup comes along, we can participate again. I still believe in the AI buildout story, but playing it differently now. - When will that happen? I have no idea. Watch this space and stocks showing up in the etoro portfolio if /when I buy them. - Been a choppy week and I expect it to continue, with a bias that we might start a short term downtrend (ie not just sideways). Coming Monday's price action might be the tell. - All the best."}}}, "rest_id": "2070613335868063767", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "616", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
the beauty of the four-year crypto cycle is that halfway through every cycle, everyone asks:

"is this time different?"

no.. 

but yes

every cycle is different

this cycle, they listed almost every asset with even a whiff of external demand or a path to a CEX

at the same time, the supply that the marginal crypto buyer was competing for increased dramatically

then the largest S-curve TAM in decades arrived

now the same buyer who would've chased alts in 2021 is buying AI infra plays on IBKR shilled to them by broccoli-cut tiktok influencers

co-packaged optics
lasers
photonics
DRAM

crypto is no longer the only frontier tech play that attracts capital, it's competing against companies for investment that are powering humanity's understanding of the universe

it's up to you whether or not you get good or get left in the permanent underclass

text:
Looks like Musk is going after photonics companies now?

Pretty positive read through for companies like $AAOI $LITE $COHR as it validates the importance of photonics. Noting new if you've been on this side of X for a while. 

Mesh Optical Technologies makes 1.6T transceivers. From the looks of it they are used for data centers and as a direct competitor to transceiver companies. Obviously, the piece of the pie is going to keep growing, and no company can have it all. So I think that this is a positive read through that Elon is recognizing the significance of optics. Considering that he owns massive amounts of compute. 

Seems like Mesh is more of a newer company, so we'll see what happens next.

text:
Robotics has been spoke about a lot this weekend. Certainly feels like the algo is pushing it in my direction.

Initially when $OUST was getting pushed in my direction months ago, it appeared to be one of the many tickers that circulates X due to group speak.

The fact it’s circulating my feed again now though.. months later, intrigues me.

Look, physical AI is the next frontier of the AI buildout. It will be the next wave.

Do I think it is incredibly early? Yes.

But early often is where the highest level of alpha is found.

Once I’ve completed researching into photonics / opticals/ connectivity I will be delving into the perspective of physical AI.

Before I start diving into individual companies I need to understand thoroughly the entire sector and when the sector is likely to move as a whole.

Do you own any stocks currently within the robotics trade? $OUST so far is the main ticker I see circulating X and of course $TSLA but this is more so product not the component required to put together the product.

text:
$SIVE  found a strategic edge hidden within O-NET : 3SP Technologies, a French specialty foundry in Nozay that now locks down the photonic AI supply chain.

This is not just a commercial partnership; it is a full-stack vertical integration strategy. O-NET serves as the industrializing force that integrates Sivers' high-performance laser IP into one of the most robust photonics solutions on the market. 

By plugging directly into this end-to-end industrial chain, Sivers is positioned in the optimal place to capture the surging demand for AI infrastructure, having solved the industry's hardest challenge: scaling photonics from the lab to the data center.

The winning equation for next-generation architecture:
$SIVE  (Design): High-performance DFB laser array architecture.

3SP Technologies (French Fab): Specialty foundry mastering III-V material epitaxy (InP).

O-NET (Scale): High-volume industrial assembly of ELS (External Light Source) modules for CPO (Co-Packaged Optics).

Why this matters for Hyperscalers:

O-NET does not just distribute Sivers; they package Sivers' designs into proven, industrial-grade modules, effectively bridging the gap between design and mass production. For Hyperscalers, this is about risk mitigation and supply chain sovereignty. 

By controlling the design (Sivers), the laser core fabrication (3SP), and high-density packaging (O-NET), this trio delivers a de-risked, "ready-to-deploy" solution for the 2027 infrastructure cycle, bypassing traditional bottlenecks. Vertical integration is no longer a luxury; it is the industry standard.

text:
🇺🇸 🔬 ⚡  
LASER EN FORMA DE RESORTE REVOLUCIONA PLASMA  

Este haz de luz gira como un resorte y abre puertas inéditas para controlar plasma de forma precisa.  

Científicos del Laboratorio Nacional Lawrence Livermore y la Universidad de California Irvine crearon el primer láser de alta intensidad tipo resorte de luz. A diferencia de los haces convencionales redondos, este rota alrededor de su eje a velocidades controlables, incluso superando la luz aparente. Fabricado con espejos de precisión nanométrica y divisores de haz, genera ondas helicoidales en plasma que podrían impulsar aceleradores de partículas compactos, campos magnéticos de más de 100 teslas y avances en fusión energética. Todo en un sistema de mesa. La investigación se publicó hoy en Nature Photonics.

En redes comentan que este avance podría cambiar la física de plasmas para siempre, otros destacan su potencial en aceleradores de partículas a escala reducida y algunos ven aplicaciones directas en astrofísica de laboratorio. Hay entusiasmo por la simplicidad del diseño pero también dudas sobre escalabilidad inmediata. ¿Qué opinas tú de este nuevo tipo de láser?  

FUENTES: Nature Photonics, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, Universidad de California Irvine, Andrew Longman, Tayyab Suratwala.

text:
i'm gonna say something that'll annoy the robotics hype crowd

most of these names aren't robotics plays. they're sensor companies, lidar shops, and warehouse automation vendors that got rebranded into "the next AI wave" by people who missed the $NVDA run

SYM is the only one here with actual robotics economics i'd take seriously. they're moving pallets in walmart DCs with real contracts and real unit economics. the rest are selling picks and shovels into a gold rush that hasn't started yet

the photonics and memory runs had actual capex behind them. hyperscalers writing $30B checks. the robotics capex cycle is still a powerpoint deck

watch who's actually deploying at scale, not who's got "robotics" in their pitch deck

could be early. but i'd rather be late and right than early and holding sensor companies for three years

text:
Almost everything was up today the space sector with $ASTS , photonics with $AAOI etc

Memory has been lagging a bit behind, though names like $WDC have gone up a lot

$MU dropped heavily midday but recovered a bit by the close

Even so, it's lagging way behind compared to the rest of the market

Everything is fine with the recent demand it received from $AAPL and others

Just a desperate attempt to manipulate memory prices because they don’t know how to adapt and are in a tailspin

On the other hand, $MU is consolidating a price range that is going to explode at any moment when some news pushes it upwards

$MU will follow the entire market, even in a more exponential and well deserved way, after the crazy earnings it reported

Very anxious to see how $MU price moves; it's quite uncertain in the short term

text:
Memo on Corning’s GlassBridge

The current CPO generation  namely NVIDIA’s Quantum-X Photonics and Spectrum-X Photonics platforms  has already been locked in and should not be affected. Any production-scale substitution would have to wait for yield and reliability validation. The timeline should be anchored to NVIDIA’s Vera Rubin Ultra platform: qualification in 2027 and validation in 2028. The first validation runs are likely to take place on Rubin Ultra, roughly 1–1.5 years from now. Qualification volumes are expected to be in the hundreds to low thousands, while validation volumes should remain below 10,000 systems. A meaningful volume ramp may not arrive until the Feynman generation.

Citi has reportedly said that the 2H27 Rubin Ultra / Kyber CPO design will be finalized within this year and will not be replaced.

GlassBridge’s real moat is the ability to integrate IOX waveguides and TGVs on the same glass substrate using the same glass formulation — not the optical waveguide itself. Other players, such as Teramount, SENKO, and Lightmatter, also have optical-coupling or waveguide-related solutions. But using a single glass sheet/formulation to satisfy both waveguide formation and TGV via formation is the part that, according to this thesis, only Corning’s fusion / overflow down-draw glass substrate can achieve.

$GLW   $NVDA  #CPO #AI #Infrastructure

text:
Evercore ISI: Semiconductors

> The rise of Agentic AI in 2026 requires heavy orchestration, memory management, and tool scheduling. Al engineers project future CPU-to-GPU ratios will expand to 2:1 or even up to 10:1.

> The firm models the broader server CPU total addressable market expanding to an annual run-rate of 75 million to 110 million unit shipments.

> Evercore models a massive $275 billion Server CPU TAM by 2030. Top beneficiaries of this structural shift include Arm $ARM, AMD $AMD, and Intel $INTC.

> This quarter, 16 chip and component companies raised prices (including TXN $TXN up to +85% on legacy products, NXP $NXPI, IFX, and STM $STM).

> Lead times for certain analog/MCU components are stretching significantly to 30–99 weeks, with backlog building rapidly.

> The enterprise storage market is forecast to grow ~9% year-over-year in 2026, more than doubling the 4% growth rate seen in 2025.

> The total data center processor market is forecasted to reach $160–$200 billion by 2030.

> Custom ICs (ASICs) are expected to capture 35–40% of that total processor market, representing a ~$80 billion market opportunity by 2030.

> Driven by this AI buildout, data center compute revenue is tracking an 18% CAGR from 2020 to 2030, a notable acceleration from the historical 13% CAGR seen between 2005 and 2021.

WFE Forecast:

2026E: Base case is set at $140 billion (+28% YoY growth), with a bull case reaching $151 billion.

2027E: The base case is raised to $180 billion (another +28% growth step), with a bull case reaching $205 billion.

Memory: The absolute fastest-growing segment. Spending jumps from $40 billion in 2025 to $59 billion in 2026E (+50%), and skyrockets to $92 billion in 2027E (+55%), making up 51% of all WFE spend.

Leading Edge Logic: Grows from $38 billion in 2025 to $49 billion in 2026E (+30%) and $59 billion in 2027E (+21%).

Trailing Node: Consistently declines, falling from $33 billion in 2025 down to $32 billion (2026E) and $29 billion (2027E).

DRAM Exposure:

> ASML $ASML & Applied Materials $AMAT lead the coverage group in pure DRAM mix for 2025E, with ASML at 27% and Applied Materials (AMAT) seeing a significant expansion up to 25% (compared to just 16% in 2022).

> KLA $KLAC and Lam Research $LRCX trail slightly with 21% and 16% estimated 2025 DRAM exposure respectively.

Credo Technology $CRDO:

> Evercore models a massive 100% growth in AEC solutions for 2026, followed by an additional 60% expansion in 2027. Credo’s stickiness relies on selling complete system-level AEC assemblies rather than bare silicon.

> The firm expects Credo's optical DSP, silicon photonics, and ZFOptics modules to accelerate sharply, accounting for ~25% of total revenue by 2027 and scaling its absolute addressable TAM by 10x to 20x.

WFE Forecast: 2026E: Base case is set at $140 billion (+28% YoY growth), with a bull case reaching $151 billion. 2027E: The base case is raised to $180 billion (another +28% growth step), with a bull case reaching $205 billion. Memory: The absolute fastest-growing segment. Spending jumps from $40 billion in 2025 to $59 billion in 2026E (+50%), and skyrockets to $92 billion in 2027E (+55%), making up 51% of all WFE spend. Leading Edge Logic: Grows from $38 billion in 2025 to $49 billion in 2026E (+30%) and $59 billion in 2027E (+21%). Trailing Node: Consistently declines, falling from $33 billion in 2025 down to $32 billion (2026E) and $29 billion (2027E). Credo Technology $CRDO: > Evercore models a massive 100% growth in AEC solutions for 2026, followed by an additional 60% expansion in 2027. text:
Major dip-buying opportunities in entire market right now:

1. $TE - Second-largest American-owned solar module maker by U.S. capacity, expanding fast, with G2_Austin solar cell production expected in Q4 2026 and 230% YoY revenue growth.

2. $SATL - Space intelligence play with a vertically integrated Earth-observation constellation . Q1 revenue grew 80% YoY, with sovereign defense contracts and Merlin AI defense constellation as key catalysts.

3. $ONDS - Autonomous defense platform across drones, counter-UAS, ISR, and ground systems. Q1 revenue grew 1,065% YoY, $PLTR partnership, and defense acquisitions.

4. $MRAM -MRAM memory leader supplying persistent memory for industrial, defense, data center, and transportation use cases. Q1 revenue grew 13% YoY, with a new $40M U.S. defense contract and Microchip foundry agreement 

5. $LWLG - AI photonics materials play using proprietary electro-optic polymers to move data faster with lower power.

6. $IREN - AI cloud/data-center infrastructure play. Major catalyst is its $NVDA 5GW strategic partnership and multibillion-dollar AI cloud expansion. Major rerate coming soon. 

7. $IONQ - Quantum computing leader building trapped-ion systems, quantum networking, sensing, and security platforms. and President Trump recently signed new executive orders focused on quantum computing.

8. $HYLN - Distributed power play with fuel-agnostic KARNO generators for data centers, defense, and mobile power. Q1 revenue rose from $0.5M to $2.8M, with Navy KARNO work, data-center LOIs, and military contract pipeline as catalysts.

9. $FLY - Space and defense company across launch, lunar landers, and orbital vehicles. Q1 revenue grew about 45% YoY.

10. $EOSE - American-made zinc battery storage play focused on long-duration energy storage. Q1 revenue grew 445% YoY.

11. $AXTI - Semiconductor materials play supplying InP/GaAs substrates used in AI data-center optical connectivity. Q1 revenue grew about 39% YoY, with indium phosphide capacity expansion.

12. $AMPX - Silicon-anode battery leader targeting drones, defense, aerospace, and electric mobility. Q1 revenue grew 153% YoY, with U.S. military demand.

13. $AAOI - AI optical transceiver play ramping 800G and 1.6T products for hyperscale data centers. Q1 revenue grew about 51% YoY, with 800G volume shipments and capacity expansion in the U.S.

14. $RKLB - End-to-end space company combining launch, spacecraft, components, and defense programs. Q1 revenue grew 63.5% YoY, with record backlog, Neutron/Electron contracts.

15. $ASTS - Direct-to-cell satellite broadband play aiming to connect normal phones from space. Q1 revenue grew from $718K to $14.7M, with satellite deployment.

16. $VOYG -  Defense and space technology platform tied to strategic systems, propulsion, Starlab, and national security programs. Recent Golden Dome award winners. 

Bookmark this post for later!

text:
The market is rotating OUT of hardware/capex-heavy names and INTO defensives + consumer staples. This is a classic risk-off signal within an otherwise mixed tape.

Watch carefully — when Tech Equipment sells off this hard, it often precedes either:
A buying opportunity in semis & photonics (our lane)
Or a broader de-risking cycle

We stay patient. Base-builders don’t panic on one red day.

Not financial advice.

honestly in my opinion it's not too attractive to me. doesn't look like it's got a lot of room to run, while having significant downside risk. already had its explosive rally, so it could potentially just trade choppy or have a slow death. then again, i think that but it still keeps going up

with that said i think the risk reward is better elsewhere. like $AMD and $MRVL. Especially $MRVL. The key insight is that connectivity, not compute, is the real bottleneck as AI clusters scale to multi-rack, and Marvell is the only pure play with end to end silicon to photonics coverage, which is exactly why NVIDIA invested in them rather than competed

My stop loss is set at 185.68 USD. 
As a major player in the photonics industry, $Corning specializes in the advanced fiber optics and specialized glass critical for scaling AI data centers. Watching closely to see if this structural support triggers the next leg up.

text:
InGaAS 1060nm VCSEL:

- About 1 dB insertion loss
- 8x100G PAM4 over 2 km (2km !!)

Take note Photonics frens bc if the VCSEL Laser and µLEDs work, CPO landscape will be different.... 

$LITE $MRVL $AVGO
embedded_text:
people usually use multimode fiber (MMF, OM3 and OM4 etc) with ~850 nm VCSELs. At 1060 nm you need custom MMF, or a ~1 um wavelength single mode fiber (SMF-28 for 1.5 um is multimode here).

guess what the insertion loss is between the 1060 nm VCSEL and the SMF? https://t.co/t8kA8HMgl5

text:
$SIVE / $SIVEF DD Thread 🧵 all credit to: @aleabitoreddit

Sivers Semiconductors is my highest-conviction laser chokepoint long in the entire photonics/AI supply chain. 

They’re not just another optics name, they’re embedded across multiple next-gen architectures that are about to ramp hard in 2026-2027.

$SIVE + Aeva likely coded from the new PR today and SIVE + Apple. Rumors / speculation.

Core positioning: Reference laser supplier for $GFS (GlobalFoundries) on CPO/NPO programs.

Primary/sole-source or heavily designed-in with Ayar Labs in the $NVDA NVLink CPO ecosystem.
Working with $JBL (and other pluggable players) on 1.6T LRO pluggable transceivers using CW lasers. This design-around of EML bottlenecks created a “relatively dramatic moat” per Jabil management. 

After the JBL PR, other pluggable makers reached out—active co-development/qualification happening now (not public yet).

Laser supplier for $POET (scale-out CPO H2 2026+), and deeply mapped into $MRVL Celestial, Lightmatter, Lightelligence, and others for scale-up CPO (main volume ramp H2 2027).

O-Net building ELS (external light sources) with Sivers lasers for Asian hyperscaler supply chains.
Not limited to scale-up CPO—pluggables, scale-out CPO, NPO, etc. Immediate revenue path in 1.6T pluggables starting soon.

AMD angle: Trendforce reported AMD is scrambling for CW laser supply to avoid being constrained by $NVDA. $SIVE is perfectly positioned via GFS reference design + direct hyperscaler relationships. $AAOI is the other obvious Western name here.

Consumer & robotics upside (cherry on top):Apple relationship likely ongoing (website still lists “Optical sensing modules for wearables” for biometric/health monitoring). Recent PRs appear to be defensive against short-seller claims the relationship ended—Sivers has probably been working with Apple for a while.
Aeva + robotics/physical AI path: Sivers lasers powering Boston Dynamics Atlas (via LG Innotek) and Nvidia Hyperion ecosystem. Possible Aeva integration.

Defense/Space catalyst: New $8.2M production order for Ka-Band Beamforming ICs with https://t.co/0oKZCt6NZc (post their acquisition by larger defense prime). This opens follow-on volume contracts in the Space/Defense supply chain—another chokepoint layer on top of photonics. Nasdaq listing & growth path:General meeting expected to confirm Nasdaq listing timeline soon. This unlocks liquidity + M&A firepower to vertically integrate downstream (make each laser more valuable) exactly like $LITE did.

$LITE went from ~$2.88B MC in 2024 to ~$67B MC in 2026. $SIVE is at a similar early-stage valuation today with far broader design-ins across the entire optical stack.

Institutional validation (huge signal): JP Morgan disclosed buying ~5.25% of the company in one month (from tiny stake to major position). $135M is spare change to US institutions—they can easily take 25%+ if float is available.

BlackRock entered as passive holder post-index inclusion.

Fidelity Research showing active positions.

This is classic retail-frontrun → institutional accumulation pattern we’ve seen before ( $NBIS, $RKLB, etc.). Float was heavily shorted by Swedish funds; big US money buying is a massive cover signal.

Why this is different from other optics names:

Markets still treat $SIVE like a pure future-CPO story, but the real edge is multi-architecture coverage + immediate pluggable ramp + ability to do M&A post-Nasdaq. They’re everywhere hyperscalers are building next-gen optical interconnects. Revenue visibility is still low because most relationships are confidential, but the supply-chain mapping is extremely strong.

Short-term volatility is expected (high-beta semis got crushed recently), but the setup into 2027 volume ramps looks exceptional. This is why it’s my favorite laser chokepoint long—qualified into the right places at the right time. Not financial advice. DYODD. This is a synthesis of public posts from @aleabitoreddit

text:
Japan just crushed 90%+ of light's quantum noise — a world-record 10.1 dB squeeze on a chip.

The fuel for a room-temperature optical quantum computer Japan wants running at 10,000 qubits by 2027. It all started with a 1998 teleportation gamble.

#QuantumComputing #Photonics https://t.co/OUf7yK19yn

I call them BAG7, have been saying this for many months. 
Follow the liquidity, capital flowing from MAG7 to capex receivers -> semi equipment, memory, photonics, compute, energy for data center. 

Investors ask” Is it not cheap enough , how can they sell MSFT and META??”
Well for institutions, every dollar invested has to compound and that is unlikely with dilution and debt to raise capital. 
One question every investor should ask- What are we expecting from MAG7 to change their profitability story while this situation persists? 
If you don’t have an answer, that’s your answer.

text:
The ~22% sell-off on $AAOI in the past month definitely hurts.

But I still like them even at $11.1B MC.

Especially as they target ~$471M monthly transceiver revenue.

Which is annual rev of ~$5.6B = prices $AAOI at just ~2x fwd sales.

Ofc you'd want the ramp to actually deliver off the back of their ongoing capacity expansions for 800G/1.6T units. 

At which point, there'd be a ~9x inflection in monthly capacity when you compare Q1 2026 to End of 2027 projections.

And that whole Texas capacity expansion is still ongoing w/ ClassOne Technology just announcing orders from $AAOI for multiple wet processing systems.

So still early, as expected.

Naturally things have gotten a bit quiet lately on the news-front given they're mid-qualification from their hyperscale customer.

When they received a new $71M follow-on order back in April from them which >2x'd their backlog for that single customer.

Just important to bear in mind that it's still early in the photonics cycle too.

With firms like Yole projecting SiPh and Optical Interconnect markets to grow at 30% and 20% CAGR through to 2030.

text:
Silicon on Insulator-based #CMOS manufacturing and #SiliconPhotonics manufacturing often start from a similar wafer structure: Top silicon || Buried oxide || Silicon handle wafer
But the purpose of each layer is very different.
👉🏻In SOI CMOS, the top silicon is the transistor body.
👉🏻In #siliconphotonics SOI, the top silicon forms the optical waveguide core.
The top silicon for #siliconphotonics SOI wafer is typically much thicker than in FD-SOI, ranging from more than 150 nm to several microns depending on the platform. Around 220 nm is commonly used as a pseudo-standard in many silicon photonics processes (There is an interesting story about how 220nm became a psudo-standard - that is for another time 😊). The BOX is also much thicker because it must optically isolate the guided mode from the silicon substrate.
So the same basic wafer stack supports very different physics:
👉🏻In CMOS SOI, thickness variation affects electrical behavior:
 threshold voltage, leakage, hysteresis, floating-body effects, self-heating, and short-channel control.
👉🏻In #siliconphotonics SOI, thickness variation affects optical behavior:
effective index, phase, resonance wavelength, coupling efficiency, and insertion loss, etc.
💡One useful way to think about wafer thickness impact on silicon photonics device performance: 
Per nanometer of geometry error, waveguide thickness usually has a stronger impact (1.5x)  on optical properties than waveguide width. But in real fabrication, waveguide width variation often dominates the total spread because width errors are usually much larger than SOI thickness errors.
Same SOI stack.  Different specs.  Different physics.  Different process-control priorities.

text:
scritto parecchio su #Sivers in Svezia, ma in mano a due indiani, che mi sembra una truffa dopo che l'hanno pompata da 5 a 110 corone
 (ho anche scritto a della gente che la pompa ("gli indiani ti fotteranno"). Oggi -20%

21 giugno 2026
Menziona Sivers tra le “mode del momento” insieme a Lite e Coherent ($COHR) per le connessioni fotoniche.
Poi fa un lungo spiegone sul perché il packaging avanzato è diventato cruciale con l’AI (HBM vicino al processore, chiplet, banda, consumi energetici). Cita BESI come pure-play preferito del settore.
Sivers viene citata solo di passaggio come esempio di trend fotonico. 

10 giugno 2026
Riferisce al video Nvidia sul CPO con Lambda e scrive:  “ho scritto un sacco di post su questi ‘photonics’ cioè connessioni basate sulla luce o CPO che sono l’ultima moda della speculazione, tipo $Sivers, $Lite, Coherent $COHR”.
Cita poi il blog ufficiale Nvidia sul risparmio energetico e sui problemi delle connessioni tradizionali. 

4 giugno 2026 (due post)  Sul forte oscillamento del titolo:  “#Sivers comunque oscilla del 20% in giù in poche ore… ci sono più tweets su Sivers che su Nvidia o Google o Micron… è incredibile la valanga di post complicati che imbambolano la gente su una società che deve ancora produrre ricavi in questo mitico ‘CPO’ dei datacenters”.  

Altro post:  “Sivers in Svezia è diretta da due dell’India – uno ha venduto tutte le sue azioni il 31 maggio incassando 100 milioni di corone… Sivers perde circa 30-40 milioni anche nel 2026”. 

4 giugno 2026
Post breve e diretto:  “the indians at Sivers are going to screw you” 

3 giugno 2026
Sul rialzo dopo l’annuncio GlobalFoundries:  “#Sivers è tornato su a bomba perché hanno annunciato che saranno fornitori di GlobalFoundries e gli influencer hanno messo dozzine di post gasatissimi… In euro vale 10 euro e 2,8 miliardi, il target degli analisti resta 0,9 euro perché perde 56 milioni…”.
Sottolinea che i ricavi arriveranno tra un anno e che ci sono già altri fornitori. 

Post precedenti (1-3 giugno)
Continuano sulla stessa linea:  Annuncio GF è solo una qualifica/reference design, non un ordine con numeri (non cambia i fondamentali 2026).  
Sivers è solo uno dei tanti fornitori in piattaforme altrui (es. via POET).  
Perdite alte, cassa limitata, restatement dei conti, rischio diluizione.  
CEO indiano e stock che sale forte per poi crollare (da 3 a 88 a 61 corone).

text:
Daily Market Alpha Recap

We scanned 39 YouTube videos, 21 Reddit posts/threads, 1,709 X posts, and 4 newsletters since the last update.

The market conversation moved beyond “Micron beat.” The more important read is what the memory shock is doing to the rest of the stack: hardware prices, hyperscaler margins, power demand, and AI infrastructure positioning.

1. Memory scarcity is now showing up in end-product pricing

Apple and Microsoft became major parts of the feed after reports of price hikes across Macs, iPads, Home devices and Xbox hardware.

That matters because it validates the supplier thesis but creates pressure elsewhere. $MU, $SNDK, $WDC, $000660.KS and $005930.KS benefit from tight memory supply. But $AAPL, $MSFT and other hardware/platform companies now have to decide whether to absorb higher component costs or pass them to consumers.

The market liked the memory winners. It was less comfortable with the companies paying for that memory.

2. The cleaner institutional trade is semis vs hyperscalers

Bloomberg framed the trade clearly: own the companies getting paid by AI capex and be more careful with the companies funding it.

This is why semis and memory held the narrative while hyperscalers looked weaker.

Long side:
$SMH, $MU, $QCOM, memory/storage, equipment, construction and power beneficiaries.

Pressure side:
$AMZN, $GOOGL, $MSFT, $META, where higher memory, compute, power and infrastructure costs can hit free cash flow.

3. Natural gas entered the AI infrastructure debate

MacroVoices added one of the more differentiated ideas: AI buildout is becoming a power bottleneck, and natural gas is the practical bridge fuel.

The preferred expression was $UNL over $UNG because UNG has more front-month roll/contango drag. They also discussed Dec 2026 natural gas futures for convex upside.

This is a cleaner way to play AI power demand than simply buying every data-center utility.

4. Qualcomm became an option-value AI infrastructure story

$QCOM discussion picked up after investor day and analyst revisions.

The bull case is that Qualcomm is moving from handset/baseband exposure into data-center AI infrastructure, with management targeting material revenue from custom silicon and inference chips.

The bear case is that this business is still unproven.

That disagreement is the setup. $QCOM is now being debated as an AI infrastructure option, not just a mature mobile chip stock.

5. Oil risk premium faded, but Hormuz tail risk is not dead

Earlier today, oil was about relief: Hormuz reopening, Brent giving back war gains, and lower crude helping airlines/utilities.

In the last update, the story became more two-sided. X posts flagged safe-passage restrictions, possible ship-strike investigation, and Iranian route-control language.

The read:
the full war premium is gone, but the tail risk remains.

Lower oil helps airlines, utilities and Asia importers. But the cleaner energy discussion today was still natural gas/power, not crude.

6. Market structure looked weaker under the surface

QTR argued that the Nasdaq giving up a strong Micron-led open quickly was a bad signal. Reddit also discussed pension-fund de-risking: overfunded pensions rotating out of growth equities and into bonds.

This fits the tape:
semis strong, hyperscalers weak, $QQQ fragile, bonds bid.

Possible expression:
long selected semis / capex beneficiaries, underweight $QQQ, or watch $TLT if de-risking flows continue.

7. Photonics got more specific

Gaetano’s Substack narrowed the optical trade to 6-inch InP capacity and yield.

The point was not “optics good.” The point was that whoever can scale high-yield 6-inch InP wins the next leg of AI optical infrastructure.

Best-positioned in that note:
$COHR

Later / more challenged:
$LITE, $AAOI

This matters because the photonics trade is moving from theme exposure to manufacturing execution.

8. BTC wrappers stayed fragile

Crypto remained weak. $BTC had ETF outflow and liquidation pressure. $MSTR and $STRC kept trading like capital-structure stress rather than clean BTC exposure.

The strongest negative thesis was on $STRC: if the marginal buyer now requires distressed-style returns, the security may need to reprice lower unless the structure stabilizes.

Clean read:
if bullish crypto, own clean assets. Be careful with wrappers.

9. Smaller single-name pockets

$BB: QNX re-rating thesis gained attention. The stock is being reframed from dead phone/meme stock into embedded OS exposure for software-defined vehicles and physical AI.

$FCEL: data-center fuel-cell thesis kept building around the 380MW supply agreement, Fairfax zoning, and Exxon/carbon-capture angle.

$SONY: Substack argued Sony is the better GTA VI toll-booth trade than $TTWO because it captures platform fees, PSN subscriptions and microtransaction economics without owning game-development risk.

$HYPE: strongest crypto-specific long chatter outside BTC, supported by fee revenue and Multicoin valuation framework.

Bottom line

The update was about the second-order effects of memory scarcity.

The market is starting to separate:
who benefits from higher AI input costs,
who pays those costs,
who can pass them through,
and who has real infrastructure bottleneck exposure.

Best areas:
memory/storage, selected semis, AI power/natural gas, $QCOM optionality, $COHR/6-inch InP, $BB/QNX, selected data-center power names.

Most vulnerable:
hyperscalers facing rising input costs, $AAPL/$MSFT hardware margin pressure, $QQQ if de-risking continues, $MSTR/$STRC wrappers, and crowded AI names without near-term cash-flow support.

$SIVEF: one scored call on schwerelos, +1,000% YTD.

The story is silicon photonics for AI data centers, laser arrays for co-packaged optics, the GlobalFoundries collaboration, optical demand riding the compute buildout. The OTC line of Sivers Semiconductors.

One call, one direction, the tape did the rest.

Mesh is developing optical transceivers that rely on light to send and receive

text:
ended negative today but feeling better than ever. mostly due to space and defense.

i need to be better at not letting price affirmation affect my decisions. tickers fading a red market for 1-2 days does not mean they're favored by Jane or other funds. in corrections, it usually just means they are lagging and stalling behind for whatever reason. 

would be up massively currently if i sized up more on dips in space/defense/utilities as opposed to energy and adjacent.

photonics looks like the better play for now even if it feels bad in the interim

Doing a little bit of research today and I can’t help but think…

CPO/Optical interconnect ramps first 2027 ($NVDA spectrum x Ethernet photonics built on cpo, $MRVL says CPO fundamental for AI scale up) $SIVE + $AAOI

Optical I/O moves closer to compute e.g. likely switches/network fabrics + then optical links around asics. Still $SIVE + $AAOI

Photonics possibly become part of the memory answer + replacement for HBM? Optically attached disaggregated LPPDR memory pools need optical I/O from $SIVE?

I like the photonics trade

text: That would indeed be a metric much closer to physical reality. However, the race toward extreme miniaturization is rapidly approaching the fundamental limits imposed by quantum mechanics and atomic-scale physics. The future of high-performance computing will not rely solely on ever-smaller transistors, but on entirely new processing paradigms. In this context, computing based on phase modulation, quantum materials, photonics, and coherent systems may ultimately offer far greater potential than the indefinite scaling of transistor technology.

text:
$COHR $LITE $AAOI

Memory wall today, photonics wall tomorrow? 

Currently, HBM memory is the loudest constraint in the AI supply chain. AI dedicated supply is concentrated in 3 players (SK hynix, Samsung, and Micron), sold out on key nodes, and accounts for ~40% of AI server BOM. Per-GPU capacity is climbing but model sizes, context lengths, and inference demands are growing much faster. This is the memory wall we are witnessing now. 

But interconnects are quietly becoming the utilization killer. Even w/ plenty of GPUs and HBM, poor all-reduce / collective performance across racks and pods tanks effective FLOPS. Copper hits hard walls on power, heat, signal integrity, and reach as densities explode. Networking already eats rising % of total power and can drop GPU utilization dramatically w/ even minor packet loss or congestion.

Photonics / optical interconnects (CPO, silicon photonics, optical I/O) are the only credible path to break the electrical interconnect ceiling. They deliver higher bandwidth density, lower power/bit at distance, and enable true scale-up domains beyond what copper or even current NVLink can do. Hyperscalers and Nvidia are already accelerating adoption. 

Here’s the twist: optics themselves are now hitting manufacturing and supply bottlenecks. There are scaling challenges in silicon photonics capacity, testing/qualification cycles, and especially upstream lasers (EMLs undershipping demand) w/ Nvidia making strategic investments ahead of time. Integration, fiber attach, and high volume heterogeneous packaging are harder and slower to ramp than HBM production lines. HBM has a multi-year roadmap and dedicated capacity fights. Photonics is earlier in its high-volume AI curve w/ longer qualification, lower initial yields, and more complex supply chain (lasers + PICs + DSPs).

During this buildout, I wouldn’t be shocked if the tighter constraint flips at times:
•  Near-term: Memory remains painful (especially for training large models).
•  Mid-term (as clusters get massive and power efficiency becomes non-negotiable): Photonics and advanced optical networking could gate deployment and utilization more severely. You can’t just “add more HBM” if the fabric connecting everything can’t keep up w/o killing power budgets or latency.

Power is the ultimate governor underneath both but optics are both part of the solution and a new constraint layer while the ecosystem scales.

Winners will be those who solve the optical supply chain (lasers, SiPh foundries, test/assembly specialists) or architectures that minimize data movement in the first place. The AI buildout isn’t just “more GPUs.” It is a co-evolution of memory, interconnects, power, and packaging. The binding constraint continues. 

Optical (photonics) interconnects are expected to be the next major theme in addressing bottlenecks during the AI infrastructure buildout. Although photonics-related stocks have been getting hammered lately, the sector’s growth cycle ironically remains in its relatively early stages, even after the initial huge run-up and subsequent corrections. Take advantage of the opportunities accordingly.

3. Infrastructure (AI Factories, Networking, Cloud)
The data centers, high-speed networking fabrics, cooling, and orchestration layers that turn individual chips into giant intelligent systems.
Retail edge: Networking and connectivity specialists (e.g., Broadcom, Arista, and related silicon photonics or connectivity names) often sit in a sweet spot — critical to scaling clusters but sometimes less crowded than pure GPU names. Data center power delivery and thermal management plays can also offer better entry points for patient capital.

text:
Jensen Huang is investing in every photonics company he can find and the reason why tells you everything about where AI is headed (Save this). 

Lip-Bu Tan, the CEO of Intel says, when he looks for investment opportunities, he looks for the bottleneck and right now, the bottleneck is the interconnect, the pipes that move data between chips inside an AI data center.

That is why he backed Credo Semiconductor, Astera Labs and Celestial AI on the optical side.

Here is the simple version of what the interconnect bottleneck actually means.

Think of an AI data center like a city, the GPUs are the buildings where all the work happens but for those buildings to function, you need roads connecting them,  fast roads that can carry enormous traffic without congestion. 

And those roads are now the single biggest constraint on AI performance.

As clusters scale to hundreds of thousands of GPUs, traditional copper wiring is hitting its physical limits and that is where this entire sector comes in.

Credo Semiconductor (CRDO) is the most direct pure play on this theme, Credo makes high speed cables and optical chips that connect GPUs inside data center racks. 

Their revenue tripled in fiscal 2026 to $1.3 billion, growing 272% year over year at its peak and four of the world's largest hyperscalers each individually account for more than 10% of Credo's revenue.

Astera Labs (ALAB) solves the connection problem between different chip types.

Astera makes the PCIe and connectivity chips that manage data flow between GPUs, CPUs, and memory without errors or slowdowns. 

Their revenue grew 93% year over year to $308 million in Q1 2026 alone.

The optical companies are where the longer-term and potentially larger opportunity lives.

Copper has physical limits, you can only push electrical signals so far before the signal degrades, the heat spikes and power consumption explodes. 

The solution is light, fiber optic connections that move data using photons instead of electrons which is faster, cooler and far more energy efficient.

Jensen Huang made this clear at Computex 2026 because copper works as long as physically possible but at greater distances and larger scale, optics takes over. 

Coherent (COHR) is the most established optical company in this space.

Coherent makes the lasers, transceivers, and optical components at the foundation of all fiber optic communications. 

Nvidia signed a multibillion-dollar purchase commitment and invested $2 billion directly into the company and their customer order books are already extending out to 2028.

Marvell (MRVL) is the most comprehensive bet across the entire connectivity stack.

Marvell makes chips for optical networking, PCIe switching and custom AI silicon. 

Jensen Huang called Marvell the next trillion dollar company at Computex 2026 and backed it with a $2 billion Nvidia investment.  

Marvell also acquired Celestial AI, the exact company Lip-Bu Tan backed for $3.25 billion, gaining photonic fabric technology delivering 16 terabits per second of bandwidth.

Lumentum (LITE), Corning (GLW), and Ciena (CIEN) round out the major public names.

Lumentum received a $2 billion Nvidia investment for laser and photonics components.  

Corning known mostly for phone glass received $500 million from Nvidia for optical connectivity work and is up over 100% year to date.  

Ciena runs the optical networking systems between data centers and is seeing analyst price targets raised on the back of the AI optics boom.

Every time a hyperscaler spends a billion dollars on Nvidia GPUs, the surrounding infrastructure, cables, switches, transceivers, optical components has to be upgraded to match. 

The smarter the GPU gets, the more the interconnect matters.

Nvidia has committed at least $6.5 billion to photonics companies in the past 4 months alone and the companies building the roads between the GPUs may end up being just as valuable as the companies building the GPUs themselves.

Follow me @MelvinInvests for more AI, semis and the next big market themes.

Lip-Bu Tan, the CEO of Intel says, when he looks for investment opportunities, he looks for the bottleneck and right now, the bottleneck is the interconnect, the pipes that move data between chips inside an AI data center. That is why he backed Credo Semiconductor, Astera Labs and Celestial AI on the optical side. Here is the simple version of what the interconnect bottleneck actually means. Think of an AI data center like a city, the GPUs are the buildings where all the work happens but for those buildings to function, you need roads connecting them, fast roads that can carry enormous traffic without congestion. And those roads are now the single biggest constraint on AI performance. As clusters scale to hundreds of thousands of GPUs, traditional copper wiring is hitting its physical limits and that is where this entire sector comes in. Credo Semiconductor (CRDO) is the most direct pure play on this theme, Credo makes high speed cables and optical chips that connect GPUs inside data center racks. Their revenue tripled in fiscal 2026 to $1.3 billion, growing 272% year over year at its peak and four of the world's largest hyperscalers each individually account for more than 10% of Credo's revenue. Astera Labs (ALAB) solves the connection problem between different chip types. Astera makes the PCIe and connectivity chips that manage data flow between GPUs, CPUs, and memory without errors or slowdowns. Their revenue grew 93% year over year to $308 million in Q1 2026 alone. The optical companies are where the longer-term and potentially larger opportunity lives. Copper has physical limits, you can only push electrical signals so far before the signal degrades, the heat spikes and power consumption explodes. The solution is light, fiber optic connections that move data using photons instead of electrons which is faster, cooler and far more energy efficient. Jensen Huang made this clear at Computex 2026 because copper works as long as physically possible but at greater distances and larger scale, optics takes over. Coherent (COHR) is the most established optical company in this space. Coherent makes the lasers, transceivers, and optical components at the foundation of all fiber optic communications. Nvidia signed a multibillion-dollar purchase commitment and invested $2 billion directly into the company and their customer order books are already extending out to 2028. Marvell (MRVL) is the most comprehensive bet across the entire connectivity stack. Marvell makes chips for optical networking, PCIe switching and custom AI silicon. Jensen Huang called Marvell the next trillion dollar company at Computex 2026 and backed it with a $2 billion Nvidia investment. Marvell also acquired Celestial AI, the exact company Lip-Bu Tan backed for $3.25 billion, gaining photonic fabric technology delivering 16 terabits per second of bandwidth. Lumentum (LITE), Corning (GLW), and Ciena (CIEN) round out the major public names. Lumentum received a $2 billion Nvidia investment for laser and photonics components. Corning known mostly for phone glass received $500 million from Nvidia for optical connectivity work and is up over 100% year to date. Ciena runs the optical networking systems between data centers and is seeing analyst price targets raised on the back of the AI optics boom. Every time a hyperscaler spends a billion dollars on Nvidia GPUs, the surrounding infrastructure, cables, switches, transceivers, optical components has to be upgraded to match. The smarter the GPU gets, the more the interconnect matters. Nvidia has committed at least $6.5 billion to photonics companies in the past 4 months alone and the companies building the roads between the GPUs may end up being just as valuable as the companies building the GPUs themselves. Follow me @MelvinInvests for more AI, semis and the next big market themes."}}}, "rest_id": "2071324368119218499", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "147193", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang at Coherent, Sherman TX:

"AI runs on compute, but it scales with connectivity"

everyone's watching the GPU race - Jensen says the real bottleneck is something else entirely

→ connecting millions of chips across a data center requires light, not electricity. copper can't travel hundreds of feet fast enough. photonics - converting electricity to laser pulses - is what makes AI factories possible at scale
→ the full AI stack has five layers: energy grid, chips, infrastructure, models, applications. a breakthrough at any layer changes the economics of all the others
→ AI could be what finally makes sustainable energy fully market-driven - no subsidies needed for the first time in history. nuclear, solar, wind - all fundable by AI demand alone

bookmark this ↓

text:
6/28/2026 Friday Recap: Bulls see rotation.
- We finally saw some rotation back into software on Friday. $FROG $DDOG $PANW leading the way. 
- $UFO another group that also got a bounce back on Friday but 1 day doesn't make a trend $ASTS $LUNR $RKLB $PL  
- The rotation has been strong into Genomics for the better part of a month. A bit extended here but a group worth monitoring. $ARKG $GH $TWST $TXG 
- $XBI $IBB $PPH $XLV has also been a recipient of that strong rotation $APGR $MRNA $ $LLY $MRK 
- Homebuilders have also been coming to life. $XHB $ITB $GRBK $TOL $PHM.
- Airlines have been a beneficiary of crude oil coming back down. $JETS $AAL $UAL $DAL 
 - $MAGS still living below the 200sma and not much worth talking about until they can reclaim their year long average. 
- US Dollar is incredibly crowded on the long side which makes me think a market pullback is buyable, but the question is "what" are we buying. Semiconductors are still base building, Photonics look at bit tired and memory names are due for a breather and starting to trade wide and loose. 
- One day at a time as you try to get reengaged with the markets. Are you comfortable buying Healthcare plan stocks such as $HUM $CNC and $UNH or are you waiting for the powerful growth names to tighten back up. 
- Hopefully everyone is having a great weekend! Back on stream in the morning!

but 1 day doesn't make a trend $ASTS $LUNR $RKLB $PL - The rotation has been strong into Genomics for the better part of a month. A bit extended here but a group worth monitoring. $ARKG $GH $TWST $TXG - $XBI $IBB $PPH $XLV has also been a recipient of that strong rotation $APGR $MRNA $ $LLY $MRK - Homebuilders have also been coming to life. $XHB $ITB $GRBK $TOL $PHM. - Airlines have been a beneficiary of crude oil coming back down. $JETS $AAL $UAL $DAL - $MAGS still living below the 200sma and not much worth talking about until they can reclaim their year long average. - US Dollar is incredibly crowded on the long side which makes me think a market pullback is buyable, but the question is "what" are we buying. Semiconductors are still base building, Photonics look at bit tired and memory names are due for a breather and starting to trade wide and loose. - One day at a time as you try to get reengaged with the markets. Are you comfortable buying Healthcare plan stocks such as $HUM $CNC and $UNH or are you waiting for the powerful growth names to tighten back up. - Hopefully everyone is having a great weekend! 3. Infrastructure (AI Factories, Networking, Cloud)
The data centers, high-speed networking fabrics, cooling, and orchestration layers that turn individual chips into giant intelligent systems.
Retail edge: Networking and connectivity specialists (e.g., Broadcom, Arista, and related silicon photonics or connectivity names) often sit in a sweet spot — critical to scaling clusters but sometimes less crowded than pure GPU names. Data center power delivery and thermal management plays can also offer better entry points for patient capital.

Our fren @FundaAI posted in May about the 2.4T (300G lanes) adoption timelines. I found it very important, and I took good note.

Now professor Damnang is hearing the same in the street about it (remember he has contact with the main players in the Industry).

The higher technology bar needed in Photonics for 2.4T , in my view, will benefit the Big Players $LITE $COHR $AAOI etc. vs Tier 3 suppliers

Next big question is.... will delay fully CPO adoption? 🤔

https://t.co/MSW5Xft2Wp

text:
🚨The best deep dive I've seen on the optical chokepoint. Worth reading in full.

The core point is the one I keep coming back to → the constraint in AI optics isn't the GPU or the silicon photonics die. It's the III-V InP laser that silicon physically cannot produce, made in a handful of specialized fabs with 18 month lead times and qualification lock-in measured in years. Own the layer they're forced to buy. 🧐

How I'm positioned across the photonics stack:

🔵 $SIVEF → the merchant InP laser itself. The scarcest node, design-ins across Ayar, $POET $GFS ✅
🟠 $AAOI → the vertically integrated US player making its own lasers and modules end to end. ✅
🟠 $CRDO → one layer over → the connectivity and retiming that the optical lanes run through. (Copper → silicon opticals in-house with Dust Photonics acquisition)

The laser is the bottleneck. The stack around it is the trade. ⚡️

Thanks for sharing Nexus Finance work @StormDirac 

Long $SIVEF $AAOI $CRDO (3 of my largest holdings) 💪

text:
Anders, this is one of the best breakdowns of the laser bottleneck. The framing that silicon is the highway and InP is the headlights gets at the thing most photonics coverage misses → the chokepoint isn't the silicon chip everyone models, it's the III-V laser they can't make on it. 🧐

The M&A-as-validation read is on point too. Watching the acquirers tells you where the locked-in revenue lands two years out. Appreciate the depth here, this is the kind of work that actually moves the conversation forward. I am RTing it for all! Long $SIVE $SIVEF and $AAOI (2 of my largest positions!) 🤝

text:
At @OpticaWorldwide  Quantum  Summit in Glasgow, one challenge surfaced repeatedly across discussions  on quantum computing, networking, and photonics: the industry needs  higher accuracy and lower loss.

As light moves from being a communication medium to becoming part of the computational fabric, the performance requirements for photonic circuits  are changing rapidly.

Quantum computing, quantum networking, AI interconnects, and optical processing all depend on increasingly complex optical paths. At that scale, even small imperfections can limit system performance.

This is why the next phase of the industry will not be defined by a single device. It will be defined by the manufacturing platforms capable of supporting the entire quantum and optical computing stack.

In his latest post, our CEO, Ofer Shapiro,  shares why the race among photonic foundries is becoming one of the most important competitions in the quantum industry—and how Quantum Pulse Ventures is approaching this challenge.

#quantum #opticalprocessing #quantumcomputing

As light moves from being a communication medium to becoming part of the computational fabric, the performance requirements for photonic circuits are changing rapidly. Quantum computing, quantum networking, AI interconnects, and optical processing all depend on increasingly complex optical paths. At that scale, even small imperfections can limit system performance. This is why the next phase of the industry will not be defined by a single device. It will be defined by the manufacturing platforms capable of supporting the entire quantum and optical computing stack. text:
According to Lightmatter CEO, Nick Harris, lasers are key and contribute nearly half of the potential revenue in all CPO technology

The industry's transition to CPO from near package optics will accelerate in the coming years, as copper is already reaching its physical limits

For a specialized company like $SIVE in lasers and photonics, this enormously elevates the strategic value of its technology

Every CEO from $LITE to $NVDA etc , is telling you that CPO is just around the corner and that it's going to be generational and inevitable

And you're shorting $AAOI and saying that $SIVE is going to 20 SEK hahahh

I'm not getting off the $SIVE and $AAOI boat

text:
Korea is putting US$1T+ behind #memory capex.

Everyone looks at the big equipment names: $AMAT $KLAC $LRCX.

Fair. BUT

The better question: who sits on the exact HBM chokepoint - less ran, cleaner, more elastic, and still checks the @aleabitoreddit playbook?

That’s why $FORM (#formfactor) is my #1 HBM pick-and-shovel name.

> Bottleneck strength: HIGH; every #HBM wafer needs to be probed before stacking; FORM sits at the test gate where yield risk gets caught early.

> Easy narrative: VERY HIGH; AI needs HBM -> HBM needs yield -> yield needs advanced probe cards. That is the whole pitch.

> Clean pure play: HIGH; advanced probe cards + wafer test exposure, without broad legacy semi-cap drag.

> Big 3 proximity: HIGH; direct exposure to #SKHynix / #Micron / #Samsung HBM ramps, with SK Hynix already naming FORM a “Best Partner.”

> Revenue speed: HIGH; FORM sits upstream of packaging; memory capex converts into orders earlier than many downstream HBM names.

> Growth runway: HIGH; Q1 FY26 already hit record revenue, while 2026–2028 rides HBM4, Texas capacity; estimate $1.6B revenue by 2030.

> Margin expansion: HIGH; gross margin is already near 49%; operating leverage supporting a path toward the 55% long-term target.

> MC elasticity: HIGH; ~$10B market cap; liquid enough for institutions, still small enough for real rerate. 

> Platform optionality: HIGH; HBM = first leg, #Foundry adds the second layer, and advanced packaging + #CPO/#photonics test can extend the story beyond the memory cycle. 

@jukan05 @ParadisLabs @ren_stocks @au_xbt @BULLOFBRITAIN

July Picks: Already Firing

$MU + $DRAM : Closing green. The AI memory trade remains strong. My view: $DRAM has a path to $100+ by late July or August if the memory rally continues.

$AAOI : $132 → $150
$OUST : $42 → $54

$IREN : Still the laggard. EOY target: $120+. July could see a move into the $70–80 range if momentum builds.

$CRDO : I believe this is setting up for a move into the $300s. After the recent pullback from the low $300s, strong revenue growth, AI networking demand, and partnerships across the photonics ecosystem could drive another leg higher.

I am STILL adding more $CRDO & $IREN at these levels.

text:
Researchers in the Bozhi Tian Lab have engineered a wireless, bioinspired "artificial leaf" that harvests light to power next-generation biomedical devices. Published in Nature Photonics, the study by lead author Pengju Li and co-first authors Mengzhan Liufu, Cooper R. Johnston, Young-Woo Pyo, Yuze Zheng, Guangqing Yang introduces a breakthrough nanoplasmonic material—composed of microscopic gold and titanium dioxide structures—that efficiently converts light into high-performance electrical energy. The team successfully used this wireless bioelectronic platform in animal models to pace heartbeats and stimulate sciatic nerves via external light activation, offering a powerful new avenue for target-specific medical therapies like nerve pain management. Beyond biostimulation, the material can even serve as a secure human-computer interface, allowing users to input information via invisible light that is instantly decoded by AI. Read the full story at - https://t.co/Jm0RhAe5VG

Beyond biostimulation, the material can even serve as a secure human-computer interface, allowing users to input information via invisible light that is instantly decoded by AI. $SIVE

Lightmatter CEO Nick Harris: “Lasers are incredibly important. They represent literally half of the revenue opportunity of all of CPO.”

He also expects the industry to move from near-package optics toward CPO over the next several years as copper reaches its physical limits. For a pure-play laser and photonics company like Sivers, this dramatically expands the strategic importance of its technology.

text:
Buzzberg Daily Alpha.

We scanned feed across X, YouTube and Reddit, Substack .

The cleanest signal was rotation into “next liquid proxies” after AI semis: space, defense drones, humanoid robotics, Korea AI capex, and select compute infrastructure.

1. Space / defense kept attracting the highest-quality flow.

$RKLB  was the strongest space narrative. The thesis is no longer just launches. Speakers framed Rocket Lab as the only public, full-stack space pure play, with the Iridium/spectrum angle creating optionality beyond D2D: IoT, maritime, telemetry, PNT, drones and defense.

$ASTS  was tied to J-LEO subsidy speculation and broader space momentum. $ONDS was framed as a long-term drone/defense compounder, with near-term dilution/warrant risk but Russell inclusion and possible strategic buyer optionality. Drone baskets like $UMAC/$KTOS/$AVAV also came up as “future of warfare” watchlist names.

2. Humanoid robotics moved beyond hype into public-market vehicles.

$CCXI  / Agility Robotics became the main robotics discussion. Bulls like the first US-listed humanoid pure-play angle, Amazon/Nvidia backing, ~$300M backlog, H2 2026 production scaling and RaaS unit economics.

The caution: some speakers openly dislike the SPAC quality/valuation but still expect a speculative run because the public-market wrapper is scarce.

$BOT also came up as a liquid way to own private robotics markups over the next 12-18 months.

3. Korea AI capex was the most important YouTube/macro signal.

Bloomberg and Korean YouTube focused on South Korea’s massive AI infrastructure plan: Samsung/SK Hynix, chip fabs, data centers, renewables and power equipment. The read-through is broad: memory, equipment, power grid, shipbuilding, and domestic Korean suppliers.

Twitter translated that into $MU/$SNDK/DRAM upside overnight. Korean channels also highlighted power equipment and shipbuilders as beneficiaries of AI data-center power shortages and government-backed capex.

4. AI compute relative value is back.

$PENG had the most interesting under-the-radar thesis: possible Middle East sovereign AI breadcrumbs through SK Telecom, MWC 2026 meetings, and Blackwell cluster integration.

$CRWV vs $NBIS was framed as a relative-value trade: CRWV allegedly trades at a large discount to NBIS on 2030 deployed-capacity revenue assumptions, while leverage concerns should fade if revenue scales.

$MSFT also reappeared as a “capex misunderstood” long: Reddit and Twitter both argued the market is penalizing AI buildout the same way it misread Amazon’s reinvestment cycle.

5. Select semis are separating from the weak photonics tape.

Broad optics/photonics was still treated cautiously, but $MXL and $QCOM stood out.

$MXL: Panther/OpenZFS validation at Los Alamos was cited as real proof for the CPU/storage offload thesis.

$QCOM: speakers highlighted automotive design wins, data-center revenue ramp, and HBC/near-memory compute as a lower-power inference architecture.

6. Single-name alpha:

$ABVX was the cleanest biotech catalyst: speakers argued the cancer/NMSC safety concern did not replicate, the label is cleaner, and UC efficacy supports a rerate.

$FCEL stayed alive as a speculative carbon-capture setup tied to the Exxon Rotterdam pilot. One speaker sold tactically to rotate into robotics, but the core FCEL thesis was not described as broken.

$BB had a strong QNX/software thread: autos, robotics, industrial automation, Canada/Japan trade mission deals, and high-margin software model.

$SMCI was the clearest avoid: Taiwan raids, chip-smuggling probe, accounting history, auditor issues. The message was simple: governance risk overwhelms AI exposure.
